name: Active Badge Location System
short_description: A wearable infrared-emitting badge system that tracked office occupants room-by-room, enabling the first identity-and-location-aware call routing and personnel directory services.
long_description: Developed at Olivetti Research Ltd in Cambridge (UK) and described in a landmark 1992 ACM TOIS paper by Roy Want, Andy Hopper, Veronica Falcao, and Jonathan Gibbons, the Active Badge system gave every staff member a small wearable badge that pulsed a unique infrared signal every 15 seconds. A network of fixed IR sensors — one or more per room — picked up those pulses and reported them to a centralized location service, which maintained a live map of who was where. Applications built on that service included automatic call routing (telephone calls followed the person to whichever room they were in) and a real-time personnel directory. The system is a canonical origin point for context-aware computing: it showed how a thin, passive-sensing infrastructure could give software a meaningful model of the physical world — who is present, where — without any AI or machine learning, just sensing, networking, and identity lookup. It directly seeded later ubicomp work at Xerox PARC (PARCTab) and the broader context-awareness research agenda.
ai_note: No AI or machine learning — the system is pure sensing infrastructure: IR pulses detected by fixed sensors, location reported to a centralized service, applications driven by simple lookup and routing rules.

images.caption: Active Badge components on a blue background — the wearable infrared-emitting badge (left) and a fixed IR sensor unit (right), photographed by Roy Want.
images.alt_text: Two electronic components against a blue background: a small rectangular wearable badge with circuit board visible, and a larger fixed sensor unit, both part of the Olivetti Active Badge indoor location tracking system.

sources.title: The Active Badge Location System
sources.publisher: ACM Transactions on Information Systems (TOIS), Vol. 10, No. 1
sources.access_notes: Authors: Roy Want, Andy Hopper, Veronica Falcao, Jonathan Gibbons (Olivetti Research Ltd, Cambridge). Primary reference establishing the system design, IR sensing approach, location service architecture, call-routing applications, and privacy considerations. DOI: 10.1145/128756.128759.

sources.title: Roy Want ubicomp archive — Active Badge
sources.access_notes: Personal archive maintained by Roy Want (lead inventor); documents Active Badge Version 1 (1988 prototype) and the 1991 Active Badge Map X-windows application. Source for the CC BY-SA 4.0 image hosted on Wikimedia Commons.

interactions.title: Your desk phone rings wherever you actually are
interactions.description: Rather than ringing the phone at your desk while you're across the building in a meeting, incoming calls are routed to the nearest handset to your current badge location — the building knows where you are and the telephone system follows.
interactions.source_label: Want et al. — The Active Badge Location System (1992)

interactions.title: The receptionist sees who is in the building on a live map
interactions.description: An X-windows application (Active Badge Map, 1991) shows a map of the offices so a receptionist can locate team members as their badges send infrared pulses to networked sensors around the building.

interactions.title: Walking into a room triggers it
interactions.description: Because presence is detected the moment the badge's IR pulse reaches a room sensor, any application subscribed to the location service can fire immediately on entry — a door unlocking, a display personalizing, a message queued — without the person doing anything.
interactions.source_label: Want et al. — The Active Badge Location System (1992)

interactions.title: Cover the badge to go off the grid
interactions.description: Because IR cannot pass through an opaque palm, users could simply cover their badge to stop broadcasting their location — a tangible privacy gesture that gave individuals control without needing a software setting.
interactions.source_label: Want et al. — The Active Badge Location System (1992)

interactions.title: A visitor's badge that expires at the door
interactions.description: Because the system assigns a unique ID to each badge, temporary badges issued to visitors could be logged into the location service with a time-limited credential — their presence visible to the directory during the visit, automatically gone when the badge is returned.

interactions.title: The location trail as a meeting-reconstruction tool
interactions.description: If the system logs timestamps alongside badge positions, the movement history reveals who was co-located and when — a raw substrate for reconstructing who attended an impromptu meeting or was near an incident, grounded in the location service's natural event stream.
